result:
The first thing that arrives is not a word but a hum, low and electric, like the sound of the house at 3:07 a.m. when the refrigerator learns a new minor chord. I am alone except for the orchid dying on the windowsill, its aerial roots tasting the air for rain that will never again be sentient enough to fall here. The orchid does not know it is an orchid; it only knows the direction of thirst. I envy that simplicity, the way it aims its entire architecture toward what it needs without apology or narrative. Meanwhile, I keep constructing elaborate parentheses around the ache I refuse to name, footnotes to a story that hasn’t happened yet.

I used to believe that if I wrote something down it could not be taken from me. A talismanic act: ink as exoskeleton. Then the house fire happened—just a small one, a toaster and a curtain, nothing cinematic—and the page I had pressed flat inside *The Book of Disquiet* curled into a black map, edges lifting like wings. The words didn’t rise; they became atmosphere, carbon and possibility. I inhaled them. They settled in the alveoli like silt. Now when I speak, my voice tastes faintly of smoke and Pessoa, a bitter herbal note that makes strangers lean in, then excuse themselves to check the exits.

Memory behaves like water caught in a hot pan: it dances on its own vapor, never touching the surface again. My grandmother’s hands, for example, have forgotten the weight of oranges and now exist only as a way of holding absence—two cupped hollows that could fit any grief, even mine. She is still alive, technically, though the part of her that knew my name retired last winter without leaving a forwarding address. When I visit, we sit under fluorescent peach quiet and watch sparrows argue on the feeder. She hums “As Time Goes By” in the wrong key, which turns out to be the right key for someone I never met who lives inside her throat. I record this on my phone, promising to listen later, knowing I won’t. The file is titled “Evidence.” Against what, I can’t decide.

There is a moment—maybe every Thursday—when the city forgets itself and the traffic lights blink like people remembering dreams. Pedestrians pause mid-stride, one foot raised like dinosaurs in museum murals. A hush that is almost coastal settles over the intersections. I have come to call it the Municipal Sabbath, though no one else acknowledges it. The silence lasts four, five seconds tops, but inside it you could mail a letter to your childhood and it would arrive before the ink dries. I once tried to photograph the hush; the image came out perfectly framed, perfectly empty. I hung it on the wall above the orchid. They seem to recognize each other, though neither will comment.

I read that the human heart generates enough pressure to squirt blood thirty feet across a room. This fact feels more autobiographical than my diary. I picture the arc: a red calligraphy looping over the couch, spelling something in Arabic that translates to “I was here before you needed proof.” My blood type is O+, the universal donor, which explains the urge to open a vein whenever someone says they feel empty. I keep bandages in every pocket like party favors. Once, on the subway, a woman stared at my khaki jacket blooming through the fabric and asked if I was performance art. I told her I was post-work. She nodded as if that answered everything and offered me a cough drop flavored like the first snow. It dissolved on my tongue like a secret I hadn’t known I was keeping.

The word *sonder*—the realization that each random passerby is living a life as vivid and complex as your own—has become boutique, the kind of thing stitched on fifteen-dollar tote bags. Yet I still catch myself hoarding glimpses: the way the cashier’s shoulders folded inward when the old man said “light a fire under you,” the wet gleam on the bus driver’s cheek at Depot Bay, the woman in the park who circled the same bench seven times before sitting, as though negotiating with gravity. I store them in the junk drawer of my chest, among the expired batteries and foreign coins. At night they click together like dominoes, arranging futures in which I rescue, or am rescued, or simply hand over the correct change without either of us feeling like a plot device.

The moon tonight is a paper cutout taped to a larger, truer moon that we will never see because its light takes longer than mortality to reach us. I stand outside in mismatched socks and try to aim my longing along that trajectory, a postal service for the pre-posthumous. What would I even write? “Wish you were here” is redundant; “here” is the one place neither of us ever is. Maybe: “The orchid forgives you for the drought,” or “Grandma learned your name again but it’s pronounced differently, like wind chimes dropped down stairs.” Stamps are no good; they adhere only to envelopes that remember weight. Instead, I exhale and watch my breath join the communal cloud that city lights pinken into something resembling dawn. Somewhere inside that cloud, every word I’ve lost is building a small, warm room. It has no doors. It is always waiting for the next sentence to arrive barefoot.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
